other_observers: Peter Derven; Peter Derven brought my attention to the bird while we were sorting through the sparrows in a clearing together.
specific_location: Clearing at the end of FR 852 on the east side of Vallecito Reservoir, just south of the Pine River drainage
description: The longspur was foraging with other sparrows and quickly stood out as slightly larger than Chipping Sparrows, but smaller that the White-crowned Sparrow. The bird had an obvious, white eye ring and unmarked lores. The supercilium was cream-colored, and uninterrupted with any streaking. The crown had brown streaking. There was no mid-crown stripe. The auricular patch was a warm, light brown with fairly even color throughout - not showing a heavily outlined edge to it. The submoustachial line was white. That white of the submoustachial line continued as a line of the same width behind the auricular patch. The malar stripe was brown and very thin. The breast was buffy and unstreaked. The chin and throat were white. The belly and undertail were white. The back was a beige color with wide dark, brown streaks. The wing was brown and had two beige wing bars. The dark, brown color of the tertials stood out. The central tail feathers were a dark brown. From the side, we noticed that the outer edges of the tail were white. When seeing the upperside of the tail, that white area was wide. The legs were pink. The bill was conical and a pale pink. The bird was slightly larger than the Chipping Sparrows and much bulkier. In comparison to the other sparrows, the bird's body was much closer to the ground as it foraged. I didn't take note of the primary extension. I suspect that wing tips on the standing bird were often positioned in front of the tail. Since the dark primary tips were dark in color as was the brown in the tail, the primary extension would have been difficult to see without specifically focusing on that detail.Behaviors: The bird was foraging on mostly barren ground with some very low, purplish creeping ground cover and some clumps of grass. The longspur was with Chipping Sparrows, White-crowned Sparrows, Dark-eyed Juncos and one Vesper Sparrow in this clearing. When the flock perceived some danger,such as a Red-tailed Hawk soaring, our movement or a gust of wind, all the birds would flush into the perimeter of this barren ground to seek shelter within the shrubs.Call: No vocalizations were heard.Plumage: first winter
